coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots fabricated by solid-phase carbonization using p-toluidine and l-cysteine for sensitive detection of copper
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Zhi
Zhou, Qingxiang
Li, Shuangying
Liu, Menghua
Li, Yanhui
Chen, Chunmao
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a label-free “turn off” fluorescent sensor has been resoundingly fabricated using carbon dots (CDs) for ultrasensitive detection of copper ions (Cu2+). CDs are prepared by solid phase carbonization method using p-toluidine and l-cysteine as the precursors. The synthesized CDs exhibited the highest fluorescence intensity with excitation and emission wavelengths set at 300 nm and 400 nm, respectively. The CDs were selective and sensitive to Cu2+ due to the static quenching mechanism. The concentration of CDs, and solution pH and incubation time were important parameters for the developed sensor. The experimental results showed that 20 mgL−1 was enough for the analysis. As the solution pH was concerned, it was apparent that the sensor was endowed with an excellent response signal to Cu2+ and provided high sensitivity at pH 12. The interaction occurred very quickly, and the incubation time could be set at 1 min. The sensor provided a two-stage calibration curve to Cu2+ in the range of 0.05–0.7 and 0.7–4 μM with a limit of detection of 47 nM. The obtained results clearly demonstrated that this facile method was fast, reliable and selective for detecting Cu2+, which would explore a prospective strategy for developing effective and low-cost sensors for monitoring metal ions in aqueous environments.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent sensors
CDs
Cu2+
Static quenching

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Challenges in Environmental Science & Engineering - Persistent and Emerging Pollutants: Identification to Treatment and Remediation for Sustainable Environments Edited by Veeriah Jegatheesan, Tsair-Fuh Lin, Shu Li, and Eldon R. Rene

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence response of CDs to Cu2+
The features of the developed sensor

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21976211 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Transmission electron micrograph of CDs. (B) Fourier infrared spectra of CDs. (C) EDS spectrum of CDs.

(A) UV–visible absorption spectrum of CDs. Curve a is the UV–visible absorption spectrum of 20 mg L−1 CDs; curve b is UV–visible absorption spectrum of 2.5 μM Cu2+; curve c is UV–visible absorption spectrum of 20 mg L−1 CDs +2.5 μM Cu2+; curve d is the theoretical spectrum obtained by adding curve a and curve b. (B) Excitation and emission spectra of CDs. Curve a is UV–visible absorption spectrum of CDs; curve b is the emission wavelength of CDs under excitation at 300 nm; curve c is the excitation wavelength of CDs under emission at 400 nm. (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of 20 mg L−1 CDs under different excitation wavelengths (280–350 nm). (D) The 3D contour photo of CDs. (E) Effect of the pH on the fluorescence intensity of the CDs. (F) Stability of CDs fluorescence.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of 10 mg L−1 CDs, 2.5 μM Cu2+, 10 mg L−1 CDs + 2.5 μM Cu2+ at pH 10. (B) Concentration optimization diagram of CDs. (C) The optimization of pH value. (D) The effect of incubation time on fluorescence intensity. The initial experimental parameters: Cu2+ concentration, 2.5 μM; CDs concentration, 10 mg L−1; pH, 12; incubation time, 5 min. The other parameters were kept constant while a parameter was optimized, and the optimal value was used in the subsequent optimization process.
(A) Fluorescent response of CDs with various concentrations of Cu2+. (B) Fluorescence intensity versus Cu2+ concentration in the range of 0.05–4.0 μM.
The interference of different ions on detecting copper ions by CDs (20 mg L−1 CDs, 2.5 μM Cu2+, and other ion concentrations at 25 μM).
Illustration for the preparation of CDs and detection principle of Cu2+.
Comparison with different fluorescence sensors developed in recent years for detecting Cu2+.

row.entry: activated carbon, sulfuric acid and nitric acid

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Spiked recoveries of Cu2+ from three real water samples with the constructed sensor.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots fabricated by solid-phase carbonization using p-toluidine and l-cysteine for sensitive detection of copper

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing, China University of Petroleum-Beijing, Beijing, 102249, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing, China University of Petroleum-Beijing, Beijing, 102249, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a label-free “turn off” fluorescent sensor has been resoundingly fabricated using carbon dots (CDs) for ultrasensitive detection of copper ions (Cu2+). CDs are prepared by solid phase carbonization method using p-toluidine and l-cysteine as the precursors. The synthesized CDs exhibited the highest fluorescence intensity with excitation and emission wavelengths set at 300 nm and 400 nm, respectively. The CDs were selective and sensitive to Cu2+ due to the static quenching mechanism. The concentration of CDs, and solution pH and incubation time were important parameters for the developed sensor. The experimental results showed that 20 mgL−1 was enough for the analysis. As the solution pH was concerned, it was apparent that the sensor was endowed with an excellent response signal to Cu2+ and provided high sensitivity at pH 12. The interaction occurred very quickly, and the incubation time could be set at 1 min. The sensor provided a two-stage calibration curve to Cu2+ in the range of 0.05–0.7 and 0.7–4 μM with a limit of detection of 47 nM. The obtained results clearly demonstrated that this facile method was fast, reliable and selective for detecting Cu2+, which would explore a prospective strategy for developing effective and low-cost sensors for monitoring metal ions in aqueous environments.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Copper ion is an essential trace element for the human body. It is widely involved in various processes of human life and maintains the normal physiological metabolism of the human body. For example, copper ion is involved in the synthesis and metabolism of neurotransmitters (Helen and Simon, 2001; Cotruvo et al., 2015). Additionally, copper ion is also an important cofactor for many oxidoreductases and participates in the normal metabolism of cells (Boal and Rosenzweig, 2009; Olivares and Solano, 2009). The lack of Cu2+ may result in diseases including anemia and osteoporosis (Wang et al., 2017). In contrast, excessive intake of Cu2+ or perennial exposure to high levels of Cu2+ may severely affect the liver and kidney, and even cause intestinal diseases such as vomiting and diarrhea (Qing et al., 2016). Human activities have resulted in a major impact on freshwater resources (Islam et al., 2021; Baaloudj et al., 2022a). The discharge of untreated sewage and wastewaters into freshwater bodies raises a number of environmental concerns on pollution from heavy metals, pesticides, antibiotic contamination, and the spread of pathogens because of their toxicities and leads to potential health risks (Badawi et al., 2022; Baaloudj et al., 2022b; Guesmi et al., 2022). Therefore, the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency requires adequate treatment of copper-containing industrial wastewater. The Cu2+ concentration in the treated wastewater is limited to 1.3 mg L−1 before it can be discharged to the receiving water area (Luna et al., 2020). According to the discharge standard of water pollutants for iron and steel industries (GB 13456 - 2012, 2012), the level of Cu2+ in the treated wastewater is limited to less than 0.5 mg L−1 to be discharged in China. The complex and diverse distribution of Cu2+ in the environment brings great challenges to on-site monitoring. Based on this, it is much vital to explore effective, low cost and rapid sensors to monitor Cu2+ at trace level in environmental and biological samples.

Until now, there are numerous methods for detecting Cu2+, such as colorimetry, electrochemistry, Raman spectroscopy, inductively coupled plasma-based technologies (Sarkar et al., 2012; Lauwens et al., 2017; Park et al., 2017; Liu et al., 2019b; Zhou et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2020; Bao et al., 2020; Li et al., 2021b) and so on. Although these methods can accurately and sensitively determine Cu2+, most of them usually require complicated operations and expensive costs. Therefore, there is still a lot of motivation to develop new strategies to reliably detect Cu2+ in a faster, simpler, more convenient and cost-effective way.

Fluorescence detection may be a good alternative due to its simplicity and reproducibility. In the past few years, fluorescent sensors have aroused widespread interest. Currently, there are two main types of fluorescent probes, that is to say, organic fluorophores and nanomaterials (Lei et al., 2015; Chen et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2019; Tan et al., 2020; Fan et al., 2020). Chen et al. explored a fluorescent polyurethane polyol with itaconic anhydride and triethanolamine as the precursors, which exhibited bright fluorescence and good water solubility. The achieved fluorescent polymer was significantly sensitive to only Fe3+ and was used as a reliable tool for measuring Fe3+ (Chen et al., 2018). Although organic fluorophores have been intensively utilized in diverse fields including medicine, chemistry, biology, and environmental science, the disadvantages of complicated synthesis, poor stability, and biological toxicity limit their applications (Hao et al., 2017; Jin et al., 2021; Banerjee et al., 2021). Nanotechnology has developed rapidly in recent years and has become one of the most versatile and cost-effective ways for detecting and removing heavy metals from the environment (Badawi et al., 2021). Fluorescent nanoprobes usually included quantum dots and CDs. The former often includes metal, metal oxide, or metallic compounds, etc. They might have high biological toxicity and be easy to cause heavy metal pollution. The newly introduced CDs have been the focus of attention because of the ultra-high sensitivity and selectivity, rapidity, low toxicity, high stability, easy surface functionalization, and low cost and other advantages. The fluorescence properties and quantum yield of CDs may be effectively improved by doping different elements. The modification of the surface structure of CDs provides energy and forms substitution defects to create more reactive functional groups (Yang et al., 2020). Since metal ions are easily complexed with specific groups like carboxyl, amine and sulfhydryl groups, which will result in significant decrease of the fluorescence intensity of CDs. Based on these phenomena, CDs have been explored to construct significant sensors for selectively identifying and detecting heavy metal ions (Szmyt et al., 2020a, 2020b; Xu et al., 2021; Zhou et al., 2021; Mu et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2017c, 2019a, 2021; Kalanidhi and Nagaraaj, 2021; Atchudana et al., 2018; Yang et al., 2016; Yan et al., 2019; Li et al., 2021a). Copper ion is considered a strong quenching agent for CDs due to the fact that it significantly facilitates electron transfer. Yang et al. prepared red double-emission CDs by carbonization, and developed a fluorescence sensing tool for Cu2+ with good sensitivity (Yang et al., 2021). Dang et al. designed and developed an “off-on” fluorescence sensor for sensing Cu2+ over the concentration scope of 0.01–0.35 μM (Dang et al., 2020). Nowadays, the hydrothermal method has been the most often-used synthetic method for the preparation of CDs. Generally, a certain amount of water is used in the hydrothermal procedure, and so slightly soluble or insoluble precursors cannot be completely dissolved, resulting in different existing forms. Different morphological precursors may participate in different reactions in the hydrothermal treatment procedure, which result in different CDs with different structures and may influence the fluorescence characteristics of the CDs. Additionally, the organic solvent used in the hydrothermal treatment procedure may also form CDs itself during the heat-treatment process, resulting in a product containing multiple CDs. However, the solid carbonization method avoids the formation of CDs from the solvent itself while ensuring the uniform existence of the precursors. So far, there are very few reports on fluorescent sensors based on CDs synthesized by solid phase carbonization. In addition, there is no report on the synthesis of CDs using p-toluidine and l-cysteine as precursors. Therefore, it is of great value to synthesize CDs with solid phase carbonization method with slightly water-soluble precursors as raw materials and explore new sensing sensors with CDs achieved using this method.

In order to design a fluorescent sensor for detecting Cu2+, based on the possible complexation between amino groups and Cu2+, p-toluidine and l-cysteine were used as precursors for the synthesis of CDs. The CDs were fabricated with solid phase carbonization and utilized to construct a novel sensor for selectively sensing Cu2+. As shown in Scheme 1 , CDs with excellent water solubility and photostability were obtained by solid phase carbonization of p-toluidine and l-cysteine. The addition of Cu2+ quenched the fluorescence intensity of the CDs. The fabricated CDs were characterized, and the related factors that will affect sensing of Cu2+ with CDs, such as dosage of CDs, sample pH, interaction time and interference from other ions were investigated.

l-cysteine, p-toluidine, potassium chloride, nickel nitrate hexahydrate, ferric chloride, sodium thiosulfate pentahydrate, cadmium nitrate tetrahydrate, mercury nitrate, silver nitrate, and chromium (III) nitrate nonahydrate were obtained from by Aladdin Biotech Co, Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Copper sulfate was supplied by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co, Ltd (Shanghai, China). The chemical reagents used in this study were of analytical purity and have not been further purified. Ultra-pure water was used throughout. The other used reagents would be found in supporting information and the instruments for characterization were as the same in our group's previous work (Sheng et al., 2021) and could be also found in the supporting information.

Carbon dots were prepared utilizing one-pot solid carbonization treatment. In short, 0.4 g p-toluidine and 0.2 g l-cysteine were added to a mortar and ground for 10 min. The ground mixture was put into 30 mL PTFE autoclave and kept at 180 °C for 10 h. After that, the resulting black solid powders were dissolved with 20 mL ultrapure water, and the final solution was centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 5 min, and then filtered using a 0.22 μm membrane filter to wipe out larger particles. After that, the solution was purified using a dialysis bag (MWCO: 1000 Da) for 1 day and kept at 4 °C.

For sensing Cu2+, 10 μL CDs working solution with a concentration of 2 mg mL−1 and various amounts of Cu2+ were put into a quartz cuvette, and the solution was added up to 1.0 mL with ultrapure water and mixed thoroughly. After uniform incubation for 1 min, the fluorescence responses of the solutions were recorded separately with excitation and emission wavelengths at 300 nm and 400 nm, respectively. For the investigation of the effect of the co-existing ions on the fluorescent sensor, copper ion was replaced with common metal cations and anions (25 μM) under optimal conditions. The cations and anions included Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Ag+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Hg2+, Fe3+, Al3+, Co2+ and SO4 2−, Cl−, NO3 −, PO4 3−, S2O3 2−, HCO3 −, CO3 2−. The whole experiments were carried out at ambient temperature and each was repeated three times.

For the sake of confirming the practicability of the constructed fluorescent sensor with CDs, three water samples were collected from Jingmi Diversion Canal (Beijing, China), Xuanwu Lake (Nanjing, China) and Fenghua River (Ningbo, China). These water samples were filtered twice through 0.22 μm membrane to remove insoluble components and stored at 4 °C. The blank and fortified samples with Cu2+ at 0.5, 1, and 2 μM were analyzed with the proposed sensor under the optimal conditions.

Fig. 1Adepicts the TEM image of the synthesized CDs. It was observed that the CDs had nearly spherical structure and good similarity in morphology. The average size of the carbon dots is 9.6 nm. The synthetic mechanisms of CDs sequentially included dehydration, polymerization, aromatization, and carbonization (Hu et al., 2015). At the initial stage, dehydration and polyerization of p-toluidine and l-cysteine yielded components with polymer-like structures. As the reaction proceeded, the polymer structure undergone aromatization and carbonization into individual nanoparticles. Fig. 1 C shows that the main elements contained in CDs are C, N, O, S, as expected. As shown in Fig. 1B, amino, and hydroxyl functional groups were observed. The broad absorption band at 3150 cm−1 could be accounted for the stretching and bending vibrations of O–H and N–H. The absorption bands at 1750 cm−1, 1600 cm−1, 1450 cm−1 and 1200 cm−1 were C=O, C=C, C–N, and C–OH vibrations, respectively (Wang et al., 2022; Zhou et al., 2021; Mathad et al., 2022). Besides, the C–H bending vibration at 745 cm−1 was also observed. The presence of hydroxyl, amino, and carbonyl surface groups of CDs endowed them great solubility and stability in aqueous systems.

The UV–vis spectra and fluorescence spectra were measured to evaluate the features of CDs. Fig. 2 A shows the existence of multiple peaks in the range of 250–275 nm. This was caused by the aromatic π-π* transition, which may be the unique fine structure of the benzene ring and its derivatives. The absorption peak near 300 nm was caused by the typical n-π* transition (Gao et al., 2020; Chang et al., 2021; Vijeata et al., 2022), which may be due to the heteroatom groups in CDs with N, O, S, and so on. Moreover, the fluorescence spectra of the fabricated CDs in ultrapure water were also investigated. Fig. 2B exhibits the FL excitation and emission spectra of the CDs and their strong emission at 400 nm with the excitation wavelength set at 300 nm. The fluorescence response depends on the specific groups on the surface of fabricated CDs. Fig. 2C and D depicts the FL spectra of fabricated CDs at various excitation wavelengths. The fluorescence emission exhibited a slight blue shift accompanied by an increase in intensity as the excitation increased from 280 to 300 nm. With further increase over 310 nm, the fluorescence emission was no longer excitation-dependent; meanwhile, the fluorescence response decreased along with the increase of excitation. To date, carbon core emission and surface state emission are generally considered the two main fluorescence principles of CDs. Generally, the carbon nucleus emission is excitation-dependent, whereas the surface state emission is excitation-independent (Song et al., 2015). The emission around 400 nm may result from the p-toluidine core, while the emission at 375 nm may be attributed to specific groups on CDs. The fluorescence intensity of the emission peak reached the highest value at 300 nm excitation. Therefore, 300 nm was used for excitation throughout the experiments.

To investigate the effect of pH on the features of CDs, the fluorescence intensity of CDs was measured by adjusting the pH value in the range of pH 1–14. The experimental results were exhibited in Fig. 2E. It was apparent that the fluorescence signal was the highest at pH 7. Under strong acid or strong base conditions, typical groups like amino and carboxyl groups are protonated and deprotonated, resulting in changes in the surface charge and significantly quenching of the fluorescent signal of CDs (Gan et al., 2020). Moreover, no precipitated and floating particles were observed with CDs solution keeping for 30 days, and the change of its fluorescence intensity was negligible, which indicated the good fluorescence stability of CDs (Fig. 2F).

There are many quenching mechanisms for CDs fluorescence, such as static-quenching (SQE), dynamic-quenching (DQE), internal filtration effect (IFE), fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), etc. Static-quenching effect is due to the strong interaction between CDs and quenchers, forming non-fluorescent ground-state complexes. The formation of the complex may change the CDs spectrum, which may be identified by an ultraviolet spectrometer (Liu et al., 2017c; Sun et al., 2021). As dynamic-quenching is concerned, it usually corresponds to the energy transfer mechanism and charge transfer mechanism caused by the crash of the quencher with the CDs, which cause the CDs to return from the excited state to the ground state (Wang et al., 2016). When the absorption spectrum of the quencher has a large spectral overlap with the emission spectrum of CDs, the fluorescence intensity decrease of CDs may be caused by IFE or FRET. If the distance between the CDs and the quenching agent is between 1 nm and 10 nm, and the fluorescence life-time of the CDs changes largely, the quenching mechanism is FRET. Whereas, internal filtration effect is the quenching of fluorescence due to attenuation of the excitation beam or absorption of emitted radiation by excessive concentrations of CDs or by quenchers. Since no new species are generated, internal filtration effect does not result in the change of fluorescence life-time of CDs (Zu et al., 2017; Liu et al., 2017a; Jia et al., 2021).

As shown in Fig. 2A, the difference between curve c and curve d was more obvious, indicating that a non-fluorescent ground state complex occurred during the fluorescence quenching process of CDs. Additionally, after adding Cu2+, carbon dots did not show other absorption peaks. This was because there was no ultraviolet absorption for Cu2+. Besides, Fig. 2A and B shows that the spectrum of Cu2+ did not intercross with the emission spectrum of CDs, indicating that the quenching effect was not due to IFE or FRET. Based on this, the fluorescence intensity decrease of CDs may be attributed to the generation of non-fluorescent ground state complexes. In other words, the mechanism of fluorescence quenching was SQE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of CDs to Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: Inspired by previous studies that certain metal ions effectively resulted in the fluorescence intensity decrease of CDs (Szmyt et al., 2020a; Dang et al., 2020; Yang et al., 2021), the prepared CDs might also specifically recognize certain metal ions. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs was significantly reduced by Cu2+. For obtaining effective measurement of Cu2+, important factors including the dosage of CDs, solution pH and interaction time were optimized. In the whole experimental process, Cu2+ was spiked at 2.5 μM. The difference of fluorescence intensity (F0–F) (ΔF) was strongly related to the concentration of Cu2+, and which was utilized as the reference signal. Herein, F0 is the fluorescence intensity of CDs solution without fortifying Cu2+ and F refers to the fluorescence intensity of CDs solution with fortifying Cu2+.

The initial value of F0 is related to the initial dosage of CDs. Generally speaking, there is a critical value for the concentration of CDs. When it is less than the critical value, the fluorescence intensity increases with the increase of CDs concentration. After this critical value is exceeded, the fluorescence intensity does not change, and even decreases. This may be the self-quenching effect caused by the high concentration of CDs (Liu et al., 2017b). At a given Cu2+ concentration of 2.5 μM, low-concentration CDs showed higher sensitivity and a narrower linear range, and the high concentration of CDs may result in a wider linear range. Therefore, the concentration of CDs was optimized from 8 to 28 mg L−1. As seen from Fig. 3B, the dosage of CDs had a remarkable effect on the determination of Cu2+. Obviously, ΔF value changed with the change of CDs concentration and increased in the CDs dosage of 8–20 mg L−1, further slightly decreased and changed very slightly with CDs concentration over the range of 20–28 mg L−1. Therefore, 20 mg L−1 was utilized in the following experiments.

The synthesized CDs are rich in carboxyl groups, amino groups, and groups containing sulfur element, which will have strong interactions with Cu2+. The properties of these groups will change greatly depending on the change of pH value (Liu et al., 2018; Ma et al., 2019). Therefore, pH value may play a remarkable role in the detection of Cu2+. As exhibited in Fig. 3C, under acidic conditions, ΔF value increased very slightly. When the pH was greater than pH 8, ΔF value significantly increased. However, ΔF value decreased markedly when the sample pH was over pH 12. These suggested that the carboxylate and S-containing groups would have a crucial important impact on the interaction with Cu2+. In an acidic environment, the hydrogen ions in aqueous solution will induce carboxylate, amino groups and S-containing groups protonated, resulting in weakened interactions between Cu2+ and CDs. Under strong alkaline conditions, Cu2+ will react with OH− which may weaken the reactivity of Cu2+ and CDs. Therefore, pH 12 was used as the optimal pH for subsequent experiments.

The rapid formation and stability of strong interactions between CDs and Cu2+ was necessary for developing a reliable determination method of Cu2+. Hence, a reasonable time is very vital. The effect of incubation time was optimized for detecting Cu2+. Fig. 3D demonstrates that the fluorescence signal was rapidly decreased when Cu2+ was fortified. The fluorescence intensity was almost constant after 1 min. This indicated that the interaction of CDs and Cu2+ occurred quickly and stably. So, 1 min was used as the optimal incubation time. Compared with other CDs-based fluorescence sensors, which require a longer reaction time (Liu et al., 2019a; Li et al., 2019), the fluorescence quenching of this sensor was fast and stable.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The features of the developed sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Under optimal conditions, the detection performance of the fabricated sensor was accessed with various concentrations of Cu2+. Fig. 4 depicts that excellent linearity was found between ΔF and level of Cu2+ over the range of 0.05–0.7 μM and 0.7–4 μM with good coefficients of 0.998 and 0.996 (R2), respectively. The limit of detection of the sensor was 47 nM calculated using 3σ/slope, which was much lower than that required for discharge for treating copper-containing industrial wastewater by U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (1.3 mg L−1). The comparison with other recently developed sensors was shown in Table 1 . Zheng et al. synthesized CDs endowed with plenty of specific groups involving hydroxyl and amino groups by pyrolyzing citric acid, polyethyleneimine and NaBH4. Because of the strong binding between amino groups on CDs and Cu2+, CDs showed a strong fluorescence response to Cu2+. The developed fluorescent sensor for detecting Cu2+ was highly selective and very stable, and the LOD was down to 5.3 μM (Zheng et al., 2019). Lin et al. synthesized hydrophobic CDs by oxidizing triolein with concentrated sulfuric acid. The obtained hydrophobic CDs could be transferred to hydrophilic CDs by hydrolyzing in NaOH solution. The surface ligands of CDs, such as carboxylates, hydroxyls, and sulfonyl groups, may undergo multidentate complexation with Cu2+, which induces fluorescence quenching. Based on this principle, a suitable and quantitative sensor for sensing Cu2+ in soil samples was developed with a LOD of 0.21 μM (Lin et al., 2022). Present study designed and developed an effective sensor, which can achieve fast and sensitive detection when the Cu2+ concentration was higher than 47 nM. These results indicated that the CD exhibited the superior properties and the constructed sensor showed good sensitivity with lower LOD, which made it an important candidate for monitoring Cu2+ in real waters.

Considering the complexity and diversity of actual water samples, the interference effect of various ions on the fluorescence quenching was studied. The influences of concomitant cations (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Ag+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Hg2+, Fe3+, Al3+, Co2+) and anions (SO4 2−, Cl−, NO3 −, PO4 3−, S2O3 2−, HCO3 −, CO3 2−) on the selectivity of CDs were studied to evaluate the detection performance of CDs in detecting Cu2+. In the selective experiments, the concentration of Cu2+ was fixed at 2.5 μM and the concentrations of other ions were all set at 25 μM. The results were shown in Fig. 5 , except for Cu2+, the presence of other ions did not significantly change the value of (F0–F). This indicated that a relatively large number of interfering ions would not affect the response of CDs to Cu2+. In short, this indicated that the synthesized CDs had a strong ability to detect Cu2+. It was worth noting that the CDs were almost unaffected by other ions in the environment. Therefore, this highly selective fluorescent sensor can be utilized for determining Cu2+ in real water samples.

With the development of industrialization, Cu2+ is released into the aquatic environment from different sources in urban/residential and agricultural areas, which directly affects human health. Therefore, it is valuable to develop a method for the sensitive detection of trace Cu2+ in water environment. This sensor was developed under this circumstance. The reliability and accuracy of the constructed sensor for real water samples was evaluated. The three water samples were collected for analysis and no Cu2+ was found. The data were shown in Table 2 . It was obvious that no Cu2+ was found in the blank water samples. The spiked samples were analyzed and the spiked recoveries of Cu2+ were in the range of 97.8–104.4%, and the relative standard deviation ranged from 1.34% to 3.92%. The outstanding detection performance of Cu2+ clearly demonstrated that the developed CDs-based fluorescent sensor was reliable for sensing Cu2+ in aqueous solution.

In this work, the target CDs were achieved by solid carbonization of p-toluidine and l-cysteine. The obtained CDs were very sensitive to Cu2+ in water environments and responded very quickly. After only 1 min of incubation, the signal from the sensor was stable and useable. The CDs-based sensor exhibited excellent ability for sensing Cu2+ based on quenching mechanism, and provided a wide linear range of 0.05–0.7 and 0.7–4 μM, and a LOD of 47 nM. The common cations and anions will have no significant effect on the detection of Cu2+. The results from real samples clearly demonstrated that CDs fabricated with solid phase carbonization were highly selective and sensitive to Cu2+, which provided alternative ideas for the development of robust, easy to operate, cheap, convenient and fast sensing sensors. It is expected that the proposed fluorescent sensor can be used for real-world sample assays in the field. However, as more complex water samples are concerned, necessary sample characterization and pretreatment should be involved.

Zhi Li: Project administration, Data curation, Writing – original draft. : Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Visualization. Shuangying Li: Data curation, Formal analysis. Menghua Liu: Investigation, Software, Validation.Yanhui Li: Investigation, Software, Validation. Chunmao Chen: Investigation, Software, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21976211).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.chemosphere.2022.136298.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots derived from Phyllanthus acidus utilized as a fluorescent probe for label-free selective detection of Fe3+ ions, live cell imaging and fluorescent ink

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Yang, F.M., Zhou, P., Duan, C.Y., 2021. Solid-phase synthesis of red dual-emissive nitrogen-doped carbon dots for the detection of Cu2+ and glutathione. Microchem. J. 169, 106534.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Yang et al., 2016

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Acidophilic S-doped carbon quantum dots derived from cellulose fibers and their fluorescence sensing performance for metal ions in an extremely strong acid environment

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Yang, G.H., Wan, X.J., Su, Y.K., Zeng, X.R., Tang, J.N., 2016. Acidophilic S-doped carbon quantum dots derived from cellulose fibers and their fluorescence sensing performance for metal ions in an extremely strong acid environment. J. Mater. Chem. A 4, 12841 - 12849.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Yang et al., 2020

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Cross-linked polyamide chains enhanced the fluorescence of polymer carbon dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Yang, S.N., Zhang, Y.M., Xue, Y.Y., Lu, S.J., Yang, H.Y., Yang, L., Ding, C.F., Yu, S.N., 2020. Cross-linked polyamide chains enhanced the fluorescence of polymer carbon dots. ACS Omega 5, 8219 - 8229.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Zhang et al., 2019

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A ratiometric fluorescent and colorimetric dual-signal sensing platform based on N-doped carbon dots for selective and sensitive detection of copper (II) and pyrophosphate ion

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhang, W.J., Liu, S.G., Han, L., Luo, H.Q., Li, N.B., 2019. A ratiometric fluorescent and colorimetric dual-signal sensing platform based on N-doped carbon dots for selective and sensitive detection of copper (II) and pyrophosphate ion. Sens. Actuators B Chem. 283, 215 - 221.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Zheng et al., 2019

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A carbon-dot-based fluorescent probe for the sensitive and selective detection of copper (II) ions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zheng, X.C., Liu, W.J., Gai, Q.X., Tian, Z.S., Ren, S.T., 2019. A carbon-dot-based fluorescent probe for the sensitive and selective detection of copper (II) ions. ChemistrySelect 4, 2392-2397.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Zhou et al., 2019

sb:title.sb:maintitle: 4-mercaptobenzoic acid modified silver nanoparticles-enhanced electrochemical sensor for highly sensitive detection of Cu2+

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhou, M., Han, L., Deng, D.M., Zhang, Z., He, H.B., Zhang, L., Luo, L.Q., 2019. 4-mercaptobenzoic acid modified silver nanoparticles-enhanced electrochemical sensor for highly sensitive detection of Cu2+. Sens. Actuators B Chem. 291, 164 - 169.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Zhou et al., 2021

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Measurement of mercury with highly selective fluorescent chemoprobe by carbon dots and silver nanoparticles

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhou, Q.X., Liu, Y.L., Wu, Y.L., Li, Z., Li, Y.H., Liu, M.H., Qu, T.X., Chen, C.M., 2021. Measurement of mercury with highly selective fluorescent chemoprobe by carbon dots and silver nanoparticles. Chemosphere 274, 129959.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Zhou and Ma, 2016

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel fluorescence enhanced route to detect copper (II) by click chemistry-catalyzed connection of Au@SiO2 and carbon dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhou, Y., Ma, Z.F., 2016. A novel fluorescence enhanced route to detect copper (II) by click chemistry-catalyzed connection of Au@SiO2 and carbon dots. Sens. Actuators B Chem. 233, 426 - 430.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Zu et al., 2017

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The quenching of the fluorescence of carbon dots: a review on mechanisms and applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zu, F.L., Yan, F.Y., Bai, Z.J., Xu, J.X., Wang, Y.Y., Huang, Y.C., Zhou, X.G., 2017. The quenching of the fluorescence of carbon dots: a review on mechanisms and applications. Microchim. Acta 184, 1899 - 1914.
